ai_summary: Elon announces an upcoming Starship launch scheduled for the following week. This represents a key SpaceX milestone in advancing their fully reusable super-heavy lift vehicle development, demonstrating progress toward operational space transportation and the company's broader mission to enable Mars colonization and reduce launch costs.
embed_html:
Starship launch next week! https://t.co/Vo2VL5AxPf

— Elon Musk (@elonmusk) May 13, 2026

Delta wanted to make it painful, difficult and expensive for their customers. Hard to see how that is a winning

parent.text: Delta rejected adopting SpaceX's @Starlink on its fleet because it wanted to provide internet connectivity to passengers via the Delta Sync portal, instead of the Starlink-branded portal. Delta has since chosen to partner with Amazon's LEO. This will result in Delta falling
parent.name: Sawyer Merritt
parent.embed_html:
Delta rejected adopting SpaceX's @Starlink on its fleet because it wanted to provide internet connectivity to passengers via the Delta Sync portal, instead of the Starlink-branded portal. Delta has since chosen to partner with Amazon's LEO.

This will result in Delta falling… pic.twitter.com/gTt8JrYx8R

— Sawyer Merritt (@SawyerMerritt) May 13, 2026

ai_summary: SpaceX's Starlink pursued a partnership with Delta Airlines to provide in-flight WiFi, but Delta rejected it in favor of Amazon's LEO satellite service because Starlink required seamless, portal-free connectivity while Delta wanted to control the user experience through its own branded portal. Musk's response reveals a key product philosophy: Starlink prioritizes frictionless user experience (working 'as though you were at home') over allowing partners to insert intermediaries, illustrating a fundamental disagreement on customer experience strategy that cost SpaceX a major commercial deal.
embed_html:
Not exactly. SpaceX requires that there be no annoying “portal” to use Starlink.

Starlink WiFi must just work effortlessly every time, as though you were at home.

Delta wanted to make it painful, difficult and expensive for their customers. Hard to see how that is a winning…

— Elon Musk (@elonmusk) May 13, 2026

text: @PTrubey Yes, SpaceX deliberately accepted lower revenue deals with airlines in exchange for making Starlink super easy to use and available to all passengers
summary: @PTrubey Yes, SpaceX deliberately accepted lower revenue deals with airlines in exchange for making Starlink super easy to use and available to all passengers

parent.text: Wow. Huge respect. WiFi portals are some of the most annoying things. Starlink would rather give up significant revenue than allow an airline to burden flight customers with portals.
parent.name: Phil Trubey
parent.embed_html:
Wow. Huge respect. WiFi portals are some of the most annoying things. Starlink would rather give up significant revenue than allow an airline to burden flight customers with portals. https://t.co/3tmtR6P4NK

— Phil Trubey (@PTrubey) May 14, 2026

ai_summary: SpaceX's Starlink deliberately structured airline partnerships with lower revenue terms to eliminate WiFi portal friction for passengers, prioritizing user experience and accessibility over short-term profitability. This demonstrates a strategic business decision to build competitive advantage through superior product usability rather than maximum revenue extraction per transaction, reflecting a customer-first positioning strategy in the satellite internet market.
embed_html:
Yes, SpaceX deliberately accepted lower revenue deals with airlines in exchange for making Starlink super easy to use and available to all passengers

— Elon Musk (@elonmusk) May 14, 2026

parent.text: BREAKING: SpaceX has once again broken the record for the tallest rocket ever built. For the third time in just three years, a new version of Starship has been stacked at Starbase in South Texas near the US-Mexico border. Starship V3 is larger and more powerful than previous
parent.name: Tesla Owners Silicon Valley
parent.embed_html:
BREAKING: SpaceX has once again broken the record for the tallest rocket ever built.

For the third time in just three years, a new version of Starship has been stacked at Starbase in South Texas near the US-Mexico border.

Starship V3 is larger and more powerful than previous… pic.twitter.com/j2rA6slUL5

— Tesla Owners Silicon Valley (@teslaownersSV) May 12, 2026

ai_summary: SpaceX has completed stacking of Starship V3, marking the third major iteration of the rocket in three years, with each version increasing in size and capability. Elon's reply indicates continued aggressive development velocity, with V4 planned to be 15-20% longer than V3, demonstrating SpaceX's iterative engineering approach to rapidly advancing rocket technology and manufacturing capabilities at scale.
embed_html:
V4 will be 15% to 20% longer

— Elon Musk (@elonmusk) May 13, 2026

ai_summary: Elon Musk clarifies that only he and NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang were aboard Air Force One, correcting speculation about multiple CEOs present. When asked if Starlink provided internet connectivity on the flight, he confirms it did—demonstrating a real-world application of Starlink's satellite internet infrastructure for critical government operations and highlighting the product's capability to serve high-priority use cases.
embed_html:
Yup!

— Elon Musk (@elonmusk) May 13, 2026
